title: Apply: Classical and Enlightenment Thinkers
description: Apply your understanding of the Classical and Enlightenment thinks and their theories in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Principles of the American Revolution: The Ideas and Events That Led to Change

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from John Locke’s *Second Treatise of Government* (1690).**

Thus every man, by agreeing with others to make one body politic under one government, puts himself under an obligation to everyone in that society to submit to the decisions of the majority, and to be bound by it. Otherwise—that is, if he were willing to submit himself only to the majority acts that he approved of—the original compact through which he and the others incorporated into society would be meaningless . . .



**Which principle is described in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This excerpt is from John Locke’s *Second Treatise of Government* (1690).**

The natural liberty of man is to be free from any superior power on earth, and not to be under the will or legislative authority of man, but to have only the law of nature for his rule. The liberty of man, in society, is to be under no other legislative power, but that established, by consent, in the commonwealth; nor under the [[☃ definition 1]] of any will, or restraint of any law, but what that legislative shall enact, according to the trust put in it.

**What principle does Locke describe in the passage?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

**This excerpt is from Algernon Sidney’s *Discourses Concerning Government* (1698).**

There being no such thing therefore, according to the law of nature, as an hereditary right to the [[☃ definition 1]] of the world, or any part of it; nor one man that can derive to himself a title from the first fathers of mankind, by which he can rightly pretend to be preferred before others to that command, or a part of it, and none can be derived from [[☃ definition 2]], or other [[☃ definition 3]], who had none in themselves; we may justly spare our pains of seeking farther into that matter. 

**Which statement best expresses the main idea of the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a king and powerful hunter in the Bible

choices.content: Governments must have consent of the governed.
Mixed governments are the most effective.
Public education is necessary for a functioning society.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Algernon Sidney’s *Discourses Concerning Government* (1698).**

There being no such thing therefore, according to the law of nature, as an hereditary right to the [[☃ definition 1]] of the world, or any part of it; nor one man that can derive to himself a title from the first fathers of mankind, by which he can rightly pretend to be preferred before others to that command, or a part of it, and none can be derived from [[☃ definition 2]], or other [[☃ definition 3]], who had none in themselves; we may justly spare our pains of seeking farther into that matter. 

**Which belief is Algernon Sidney challenging in the excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a king and powerful hunter in the Bible

choices.content: Every person is born with natural rights.
Kings have a divine right to rule.
Governments must have consent of the governed.
The right to vote is fundamental.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Aristotle’s *Politics.***

Of forms of government in which one rules, we call that which regards the common interests, kingship or royalty; that in which more than one, but not many, rule, aristocracy; and it is so called, either because the rulers are the best men, or because they have at heart the best interests of the state and of the citizens. But when the citizens at large administer the state for the common interest, the government is called by the generic name—a constitution. And there is a reason for this use of language. One man or a few may excel in virtue; but as the number increases it becomes more difficult for them to attain perfection in every kind of virtue, though they may in military virtue, for this is found in the masses. Hence in a constitutional government the fighting-men have the supreme power, and those who possess arms are the citizens.

Of the above-mentioned forms, the [[☃ definition 2]] are as follows: of royalty, tyranny; of aristocracy, oligarchy; of constitutional government, democracy. For tyranny is a kind of monarchy which has in view the interest of the monarch only; [[☃ definition 1]] has in view the interest of the wealthy; democracy, of the needy: none of them the common good of all.

**Which statement best expresses the warning Aristotle gives in the excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: rule by only a few people
corruptions; changes from the proper form of something to an improper or bad form

choices.content: Men are not perfect and are easily corrupted when one or few are put in charge of the government.
To create a democracy is the ultimate insult to God, as kings are chosen through divine right.
A constitution is a form of contract in which citizens agree to give up some freedom for government protection.
Each form of government, when taken to the extreme, can turn into a corrupt version of itself.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Aristotle’s *Politics.***

In the third place is a combination of the two systems, taking some features from the [[☃ definition 1]] law and some from the democratic; I mean, for example, that it is thought to be democratic for the offices to be assigned by lot, for them to be elected oligarchic, and democratic for them not to have a property-qualification, oligarchic to have one; therefore it is aristocratic and constitutional to take one feature from one form and the other from the other, from oligarchy that offices are to be elected, and from democracy that this is not to be on a property-qualification. 

This then is the mode of the mixture; and the mark of a good mixture of democracy and oligarchy is when it is possible to speak of the same constitution as a democracy and as an oligarchy; for manifestly this is so when it is said because they have been mixed well, and this is the case with the form that lies in the middle, for each of the two extreme forms can be seen in it.


**Which  idea is described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to a government in which only a few people hold all the power

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Cicero’s *Tusculan Disputations.***

​​If, then, there is such a thing as a happy life, it is to be gloried in, spoken of, and [[☃ definition 1]] by the person who enjoys it . . .

Now, unless an honorable life is a happy life, there must, of course, be something preferable to a happy life; for that which is honorable all men will certainly grant to be preferable to anything else. And thus there will be something better than a happy life: but what can be more absurd than such an assertion? What! when they grant vice to be [[☃ definition 2]] at [[☃ definition 3]] life miserable, must they not admit that there is a corresponding power in virtue to make life happy? For contraries follow from contraries. 

And here I ask what weight they think there is in the balance of Critolaus, who having put the goods of the mind into one scale, and the goods of the body and other external advantages into the other, thought the goods of the mind outweighed the others so far that they would require the whole earth and sea to equalize the scale.

**According to Cicero, what leads a person to happiness?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from Cicero’s *De Re Publica* (54–51 B.C.E.).**

Therefore, since law is the bond which unites the civic association, and the justice enforced by law is the same for all, by what justice can an association of citizens be held together when there is no equality among the citizens? For if we cannot agree to equalize men's wealth, and equality of [[☃ definition 1]] ability is impossible, the legal rights at least of those who are citizens of the same commonwealth ought to be equal. For what is a State except an association or partnership in justice?

**Which statement best summarizes Cicero’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: natural and having existed from birth

choices.content: Good governments ensure that all citizens have the same amount of money and power.
Strong republics require all citizens to have equal voting rights to choose their representatives.
Laws must be applied fairly to all people for a stable government to exist.
Governments that succeed are ones that have harsh punishments for crimes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from one of Cicero’s writings. It was written between 54 and 51 B.C.E.**

If [the State] leaves [the selection of its rulers] to chance, it will be as quickly overturned as a ship whose pilot should be chosen [[☃ definition 1]] from among the passengers. But if a free people chooses the men to whom it is entrust its fortunes, and, since it desires its own safety, chooses the best men, then certainly the safety of the State depends upon the wisdom of its best men, especially since Nature has provided not only that those men who are superior in virtue and in spirit should rule the weaker, but also the weaker should be willing to obey the stronger.

**What type of government does Cicero advocate for in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from one of Cicero’s writings. It was written between 54 and 51 B.C.E.**

If [the State] leaves [the selection of its rulers] to chance, it will be as quickly overturned as a ship whose pilot should be chosen [[☃ definition 1]] from among the passengers. But if a free people chooses the men to whom it is entrust its fortunes, and, since it desires its own safety, chooses the best men, then certainly the safety of the State depends upon the wisdom of its best men, especially since Nature has provided not only that those men who are superior in virtue and in spirit should rule the weaker, but also the weaker should be willing to obey the stronger.

**In this excerpt, Cicero argues that “a free people” should select its rulers based on what characteristic(s)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Their large fortunes and high status

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Algernon Sidney’s *Discourses Concerning Government* (1683).**

We must look for another rule of our obedience, and shall find that to be the law, which being, as I said before, *[[☃ definition 1]]*, must be founded upon that eternal principle of reason and truth, from whence the rule of justice which is sacred and pure ought to be [[☃ definition 2]], and not from the [[☃ definition 3]] will of man, which [[☃ definition 4]] according to the different interests, [[☃ definition 5]] and passions that at several times reign in several nations, one day [[☃ definition 6]] what had been enacted the other. 

The [[☃ definition 7]] therefore that deserves the name of a law, which derives its excellency not from antiquity, or from the dignity of the legislators, but from an [[☃ definition 8]] equity and justice, ought to be made in pursuance of that universal reason to which all nations at all times owe an equal [[☃ definition 9]] and obedience.

**Which statement best summarizes Sidney’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The king’s right to rule comes from a divine power, making monarchs the ultimate authority in a nation.
The people will follow only those laws that call for severe penalties, as people follow laws only when they fear punishment.
The law must be flexible so that it can be responsive to the people’s changing interests, passions, and values.
The people should follow only laws that are based on reason and fairness, not laws that frequently change for arbitrary reasons.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Algernon Sidney’s *Discourses Concerning Government* (1683).**

As liberty consists only in being subject to no man’s will, and nothing [[☃ definition 1]] a slave but a dependence upon the will of another; if there be no other law in a kingdom but the will of a prince, there is no such thing as liberty.

**Which principle does Sidney state is necessary for true liberty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: indicates; serves as a sign of something
